# str_length

<center>
![](images/stringr_str_length.png){style="width:200px"}
</center>

The function `stringr::str_length()` allows you to determine the length of a string.
This will give you the number of characters each string has.

[Tidyverse reference page](https://stringr.tidyverse.org/reference/str_length.html)

## str_length

First we'll count the number of characters in one string (a scalar).


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
stringr::str_length("abc")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 3
```


:::
:::


Next we can count the number of characters within each string of a vector.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#Create a vector of strings
vec <- c("abc", "defghijk", "lmnopqrstuvwxyz")
#Count with a vector
stringr::str_length(vec)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1]  3  8 15
```


:::
:::


## White spaces

What about counting white spaces such as spaces (`" "`), tabs (`\t`), and new spaces (`\n`)?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#Create a vector of strings
vec <- c("gataac", "gat aac", "gat\taac", "gat\naac")
```
:::


View the strings with [`stringr::str_view()`](/stringr/str_view.qmd).

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#View the strings
stringr::str_view(vec)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] │ gataac
[2] │ gat aac
[3] │ gat{\t}aac
[4] │ gat
    │ aac
```


:::
:::


Count the characters in the strings.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#Count characters of strings
stringr::str_length(vec)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 6 7 7 7
```


:::
:::


You'll notice that these three white spaces count as 1 character.
